---
title: "NCBN Thermal Report"
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
editor options: 
  markdown:
    wrap: none
output: 
  html_document:
    keep_md: false
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    fig_caption: true
    number_sections: false
    df_print: kable
    theme: journal
    css: journalnps.min.css
    includes:
        before_body:
          - header.html
date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
params:
  park: "CACO" #change to FIIS or CACO when knitting for park specific report
  park_name: "Cape Cod National Seashore"
---

```{=html}
<style>
p.caption {
  font-size: 0.8em;
}
</style>
```

```{r setup, include=FALSE}

# Libraries (load once)
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(ggrepel)
library(glue)
library(fs)
library(scales)   # for label_number
library(here) # use to load data from relative paths
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

options(knitr.table.format = "html")

# -------------------------------
# 0) GLOBAL STYLE + PALETTE
# -------------------------------
theme_set(theme_classic(base_size = 14))

# Locked legend order for Park_Code
park_levels <- c("ASIS", "FIIS", "CACO")

# Consistent colors and shapes everywhere
park_pal    <- c("ASIS"="#1f77b4","FIIS"="#ff7f00","CACO"="#2ca02c")
park_shapes <- c("ASIS"=16,"FIIS"=17,"CACO"=15)

# Accent colors for thresholds/extreme markers (not mapped to Park_Code)
grey_grid     <- "grey40"
accent_red    <- "#d62728"
accent_blue   <- "#1f78b4"
accent_purple <- "#8b3fb9"

# Reusable scales—use these in every plot that maps color/fill/shape to Park_Code
scale_park_color <- function(...) scale_color_manual(values = park_pal, breaks = park_levels, ...)
scale_park_fill  <- function(...) scale_fill_manual(values  = park_pal, breaks = park_levels, ...)
scale_park_shape <- function(...) scale_shape_manual(values = park_shapes, breaks = park_levels, ...)
```

```{r Load-Data, include=FALSE}
dat <- read_excel(here::here("data", "Site_Summary_Master_b.xlsx"), sheet = "Site_Year") %>%
  rename_with(~ gsub(" ", "_", .)) %>%
  mutate(
    Park_Code              = toupper(Park_Code),
    Park_Code              = factor(Park_Code, levels = park_levels), # lock legend order
    Mean_HDH_daily         = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(Mean_HDH_daily)),
    Mean_CDH_daily         = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(Mean_CDH_daily)),
    `%_abv_thres`          = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(`%_abv_thres`)),
    Number_warming_events  = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(Number_warming_events)),
    Year                   = suppressWarnings(as.integer(Year)),
    Transect               = toupper(gsub("[^A-Za-z]", "", trimws(Transect))),
    long_term_site = if_else(Site_Name %in% c("East Harbor", "Provincetown Harbor"), FALSE, TRUE)
  )


```

```{r helper-functions, include=FALSE}
# Site-year (avg across transects) -> Site-level (avg across years)
make_site_level <- function(df, metrics = c("Mean_HDH_daily","Mean_CDH_daily","%_abv_thres")) {
  df %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Park_Code, Site_Name, Year) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      HDH_year     = if ("Mean_HDH_daily" %in% metrics) mean(Mean_HDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE) else NA_real_,
      CDH_year     = if ("Mean_CDH_daily" %in% metrics) mean(Mean_CDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE) else NA_real_,
      pct_hot_year = if ("%_abv_thres"    %in% metrics) mean(`%_abv_thres`,    na.rm = TRUE) else NA_real_,
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Park_Code, Site_Name) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      mean_HDH = mean(HDH_year,     na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_CDH = mean(CDH_year,     na.rm = TRUE),
      pct_hot  = mean(pct_hot_year, na.rm = TRUE),
      n_years  = dplyr::n_distinct(Year),
      .groups  = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Label = paste0(Site_Name, " (n=", n_years, ")"))
}

# Flag extreme years per site (e.g., 90th percentile of metric within each site)
flag_extreme_by_site <- function(df, value_col, prob = 0.9, flag_name = "Extreme") {
  df %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Site_Name) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      thresh = stats::quantile(.data[[value_col]], prob, na.rm = TRUE),
      "{flag_name}" := .data[[value_col]] >= .data[["thresh"]]
    ) %>%
    dplyr::ungroup()
  
}
```

```{r filter-to-park, include=FALSE}
park_code <- params$park
dat_pk <- dat %>% filter(Park_Code == park_code)
```

```{r Per-Park-Plotting, include=FALSE}
# A) High Heat Years (HDH) by site (facetted), one park
plot_hdh_extreme_by_site <- function(df, park_code) {
  site_year <- df %>%
    dplyr::filter(Park_Code == park_code) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Site_Name, Year) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(Mean_HDH = mean(Mean_HDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    flag_extreme_by_site(value_col = "Mean_HDH", prob = 0.9, flag_name = "Extreme")
  
  ggplot(site_year, aes(x = Year, y = Mean_HDH)) +
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
    geom_point(size = 2) +
    geom_point(data = subset(site_year, Extreme), color = accent_red, size = 3.5) +
    geom_text_repel(
      data = subset(site_year, Extreme),
      aes(label = Year), color = accent_red, size = 4, show.legend = FALSE,
      nudge_x = 0.4, nudge_y = 0.6, hjust = 0, direction = "y",
      box.padding = 0.3, point.padding = 0.2
    ) +
    geom_hline(aes(yintercept = thresh), linetype = "dashed", color = accent_red) +
    facet_wrap(~ Site_Name, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(accuracy = 1),
                       expand = expansion(mult = c(0.1, 0.2))) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(site_year$Year, na.rm = TRUE), max(site_year$Year, na.rm = TRUE), by = 2)) +
    labs(
      title    = glue("Mean daily HDH by site ({park_code})"),
      subtitle = "Red dashed line = site-specific 90th percentile value; red points = extreme high heat years",
      x = "Year", y = "Mean daily heating degree hours"
    )
}

# B) Reduced Cooling Years (CDH) by site (facetted), one park
plot_cdh_lowcool_by_site <- function(df, park_code) {
  site_year <- df %>%
    dplyr::filter(Park_Code == park_code) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Site_Name, Year) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(Mean_CDH = mean(Mean_CDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    flag_extreme_by_site(value_col = "Mean_CDH", prob = 0.9, flag_name = "Low_Cooling")
  
  ggplot(site_year, aes(x = Year, y = Mean_CDH)) +
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
    geom_point(size = 2) +
    geom_point(data = subset(site_year, Low_Cooling), color = accent_blue, size = 3.5) +
    geom_text_repel(
      data = subset(site_year, Low_Cooling),
      aes(label = Year), color = accent_blue, size = 4, show.legend = FALSE,
      nudge_x = 0.4, nudge_y = 0.6, hjust = 0, direction = "y",
      box.padding = 0.3, point.padding = 0.2
    ) +
    geom_hline(aes(yintercept = thresh), linetype = "dashed", color = accent_blue) +
    facet_wrap(~ Site_Name, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(accuracy = 1),
                       expand = expansion(mult = c(0.1, 0.2))) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(site_year$Year, na.rm = TRUE), max(site_year$Year, na.rm = TRUE), by = 2)) +
    labs(
      title    = glue("Mean daily CDH by site ({park_code})"),
      subtitle = "Blue dashed line = site-specific 90th percentile value; blue points = extreme low cooling years",
      x = "Year", y = "Mean daily cooling degree hours"
    )
}

# C) Double-Stress years (HDH high & CDH high), one park
plot_double_stress_by_site <- function(df, park_code) {
  site_year <- df %>%
    dplyr::filter(Park_Code == park_code) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Site_Name, Year) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      Mean_HDH = mean(Mean_HDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE),
      Mean_CDH = mean(Mean_CDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Site_Name) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      hdh_thresh = stats::quantile(Mean_HDH, 0.9, na.rm = TRUE),
      cdh_thresh = stats::quantile(Mean_CDH, 0.9, na.rm = TRUE),
      High_Heat   = Mean_HDH >= hdh_thresh,
      Low_Cooling = Mean_CDH >= cdh_thresh,
      Double_Stress = High_Heat & Low_Cooling
    ) %>% dplyr::ungroup()
  
  ggplot(site_year, aes(x = Year, y = Mean_HDH)) +
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
    geom_point(size = 2) +
    geom_point(data = subset(site_year, Double_Stress), color = accent_purple, size = 4) +
    geom_text_repel(
      data = subset(site_year, Double_Stress),
      aes(label = Year), color = accent_purple, size = 4, show.legend = FALSE,
      nudge_x = 0.4, nudge_y = 0.6, hjust = 0, direction = "y",
      box.padding = 0.3, point.padding = 0.2
    ) +
    geom_hline(aes(yintercept = hdh_thresh), linetype = "dashed", color = accent_red) +
    facet_wrap(~ Site_Name, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(accuracy = 1),
                       expand = expansion(mult = c(0.1, 0.2))) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(site_year$Year, na.rm = TRUE), max(site_year$Year, na.rm = TRUE), by = 2)) +
    labs(
      title    = glue("Compound stress years by site ({park_code})"),
      subtitle = "Purple points = years with extreme high heat AND low cooling",
      x = "Year", y = "Mean daily heating degree hours"
    )
}
```

```{r Regional-PLotting-Functions, include=FALSE}
# D) Site-level thermal stress across years (lines colored by Park_Code)
plot_sitelevel_thermal_stress_lines <- function(df) {
  site_year <- df %>%
    dplyr::filter(Park_Code %in% park_levels) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Park_Code, Site_Name, Year) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(Mean_HDH = mean(Mean_HDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  
  threshold <- stats::quantile(site_year$Mean_HDH, 0.9, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  site_labels <- site_year %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Site_Name) %>%
    dplyr::filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
    dplyr::ungroup()
  
  ggplot(site_year,
         aes(x = Year, y = Mean_HDH, group = Site_Name, color = Park_Code)) +
    geom_line(alpha = 0.7, linewidth = 1) +
    geom_point(size = 2) +
    geom_text_repel(
      data = site_labels,
      aes(label = Site_Name),
      size = 3.5, direction = "y", hjust = 0,
      nudge_x = 1, box.padding = 0.5, point.padding = 0.3,
      segment.color = "grey60", show.legend = FALSE
    ) +
    geom_point(
      data = subset(site_year, Mean_HDH >= threshold),
      color = accent_red, size = 3
    ) +
    geom_text_repel(
      data = subset(site_year, Mean_HDH >= threshold),
      aes(label = Year),
      size = 3, color = accent_red, show.legend = FALSE
    ) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = threshold, linetype = "dashed", color = accent_red) +
    scale_park_color() +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(site_year$Year, na.rm = TRUE),
                                    max(site_year$Year, na.rm = TRUE), by = 2)) +
    expand_limits(x = max(site_year$Year, na.rm = TRUE) + 8) +
    labs(
      title    = "Site-Level Thermal Stress Across Years",
      subtitle = "Lines = sites; labels show site identity and extreme heat years",
      x = "Year", y = "Mean Heating Degree Hours per Day",
      color = "Park"
    )
}

# E) Thermal stress vs cooling (site-level scatter with size = % hot)
plot_stress_vs_cooling <- function(df) {
  site_level <- make_site_level(df, metrics = c("Mean_HDH_daily","Mean_CDH_daily","%_abv_thres"))
  median_HDH <- 8.8
  median_CDH <- -94.9
  
  x_low  <- median_HDH * 0.5
  x_high <- max(site_level$mean_HDH, na.rm = TRUE) * 0.8
  y_low  <- min(site_level$mean_CDH, na.rm = TRUE) * 0.8
  y_high <- median_CDH * 0.8
  
  plot <- ggplot(site_level, aes(x = mean_HDH, y = mean_CDH, color = Park_Code, size = pct_hot)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.9) +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = Label), size = 4) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = median_HDH, linetype = "dashed", color = grey_grid) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = median_CDH, linetype = "dashed", color = grey_grid) +
    expand_limits(x = max(site_level$mean_HDH, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.2,
                  y = min(site_level$mean_CDH, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.2) +
    scale_park_color() +
    guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(alpha = 1, size = 4))) +
    labs(
      title = "Thermal Stress vs Cooling Relief Across Coastal Monitoring Sites",
      subtitle = "Site-level averages (transects averaged within year, then across years)",
      x = "Mean Heating Degree Hours per Day (Thermal Stress)",
      y = "Mean Cooling Degree Hours per Day (Thermal Recovery)",
      color = "Park",
      size  = "% Time Above Threshold"
    )
  
  plot_build <- ggplot_build(plot)
  x_limits <- plot_build$layout$panel_params[[1]]$x$continuous_range
  y_limits <- plot_build$layout$panel_params[[1]]$y$continuous_range
  
  plot +
    annotate("text", x = x_limits[1] + ((median_HDH - x_limits[1])/2), y = median_CDH + ((y_limits[2] - median_CDH)/2), vjust = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, label = "Stable Systems", size = 4.5, fontface = "bold") +
    annotate("text", x = x_limits[1] + ((median_HDH - x_limits[1])/2), y = y_limits[1] + ((median_CDH - y_limits[1])/2), vjust = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, label = "Thermal Refugia", size = 4.5, fontface = "bold") +
    annotate("text", x = median_HDH + ((x_limits[2] - median_HDH)/2), y = median_CDH + ((y_limits[2] - median_CDH)/2), vjust = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, label = "Chronic Heat Stress", size = 4.5, fontface = "bold") +
    annotate("text", x = median_HDH + ((x_limits[2] - median_HDH)/2), y = y_limits[1] + ((median_CDH - y_limits[1])/2), vjust = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, label = "Stress Buffered Systems", size = 4.5, fontface = "bold")
  
}

# F) Thermal balance (exposure vs cooling, site-level)
plot_thermal_balance <- function(df) {
  thermal_balance <- df %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Park_Code, Site_Name, Year) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      Exposure_year = mean(`%_abv_thres`, na.rm = TRUE),
      Cooling_year  = mean(Mean_CDH_daily,  na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Park_Code, Site_Name) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      MeanExposure = mean(Exposure_year, na.rm = TRUE),
      MeanCooling  = mean(Cooling_year,  na.rm = TRUE),
      n_years      = dplyr::n_distinct(Year),
      .groups      = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Label = paste0(Site_Name, " (n=", n_years, ")"))
  
  ggplot(thermal_balance, aes(x = MeanExposure, y = MeanCooling, color = Park_Code)) +
    geom_point(size = 4, alpha = 0.9) +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = Label), size = 3) +
    scale_park_color() +
    labs(
      title    = "Thermal Balance Across Coastal Monitoring Sites",
      subtitle = "Exposure vs cooling using consistent site-level averaging",
      x = "Mean % Time Above Threshold (Thermal Exposure)",
      y = "Mean Cooling Degree Hours (Thermal Recovery)",
      color = "Park"
    )
}

# G) Heat Event Structure (site-level; optional interpretive quadrants)
plot_heat_event_structure_site <- function(df) {
  site_level_events <- df %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Park_Code, Site_Name, Year) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      Events_year   = mean(Number_warming_events, na.rm = TRUE),
      Exposure_year = mean(`%_abv_thres`,        na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Park_Code, Site_Name) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      MeanEvents   = mean(Events_year,   na.rm = TRUE),
      MeanExposure = mean(Exposure_year, na.rm = TRUE),
      n_years      = dplyr::n_distinct(Year),
      .groups      = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Label = paste0(Site_Name, " (n=", n_years, ")"))
  
  cluster_df <- site_level_events %>% # create df with only Events and Exposure for fitting k-means
    mutate(id = paste0(Park_Code, "_", Site_Name)) %>%
    column_to_rownames(var = "id") %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    dplyr::select(MeanEvents, MeanExposure)
  
  km_fit <- kmeans(cluster_df, centers = 4, nstart = 2) # fit k-means clusters
  event_split <- mean(km_fit$centers[1:4])
  exposure_split <- mean(km_fit$centers[5:8])
  
  plot <- ggplot(site_level_events, aes(x = MeanEvents, y = MeanExposure, color = Park_Code, shape = Park_Code)) +
    geom_point(size = 4.5) +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = Label), size = 3, max.overlaps = 30) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = event_split,   linetype = "dashed", color = grey_grid) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = exposure_split,linetype = "dashed", color = grey_grid) +
    scale_park_color() +
    scale_park_shape() +
    labs(
      title    = "Heat Event Structure Across Coastal Parks",
      subtitle = "Points represent site-level averages across monitoring years",
      x = "Mean Number of Warming Events (Frequency)",
      y = "Mean % Time Above Threshold (Duration)",
      color = "Park", shape = "Park"
    )
  
  plot_build <- ggplot_build(plot)
  x_limits <- plot_build$layout$panel_params[[1]]$x$continuous_range
  y_limits <- plot_build$layout$panel_params[[1]]$y$continuous_range
  
  plot +
    annotate("text", x = x_limits[1] + ((event_split - x_limits[1])/2), y = exposure_split + ((y_limits[2] - exposure_split)/2), vjust = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, label = "Episodic Heat Waves", size = 4) +
    annotate("text", x = x_limits[1] + ((event_split - x_limits[1])/2), y = y_limits[1] + ((exposure_split - y_limits[1])/2), vjust = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, label = "Low Exposure", size = 4) +
    annotate("text", x = event_split + ((x_limits[2] - event_split)/2), y = exposure_split + ((y_limits[2] - exposure_split)/2), vjust = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, label = "Highly Stressed", size = 4) +
    annotate("text", x = event_split + ((x_limits[2] - event_split)/2), y = y_limits[1] + ((exposure_split - y_limits[1])/2), vjust = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, label = "Chronic Warming", size = 4)
}

# (Optional) H) Thermal balance at TRANSECT level (no interpretation grids)
plot_thermal_balance_transect <- function(df) {
  thermal_balance <- df %>%
    dplyr::filter(Park_Code %in% park_levels) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Park_Code, Site_Name, Transect) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      MeanExposure = mean(`%_abv_thres`,    na.rm = TRUE),
      MeanCooling  = mean(Mean_CDH_daily,   na.rm = TRUE),
      n_years      = dplyr::n(),
      .groups      = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Label = paste(Site_Name, Transect))
  
  ggplot(thermal_balance, aes(x = MeanExposure, y = MeanCooling, color = Park_Code)) +
    geom_point(size = 4) +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = Label), size = 3) +
    scale_park_color() +
    labs(
      title = "Thermal Balance Across Parks (Transect Level)",
      x = "Mean % Time Above Threshold",
      y = "Mean Cooling Degree Hours",
      color = "Park"
    )
}

```

# Background

## Eelgrass Importance and Vulnerability

Eelgrass (*Zostera marina*) is a foundational marine plant that supports healthy coastal ecosystems. It improves water quality, stabilizes sediments, provides habitat for fish and invertebrates, and acts as a globally important carbon sink ([Novak et al. 2023](#refs)). When eelgrass meadows decline, the carbon they store can be released back into the water column ([Berger et al. 2024](#refs)). Because eelgrass responds quickly to changes in its environment, it is often considered a “canary in the coal mine” for coastal health ([Orth et al. 2006](#refs)). Its ecological significance is one reason the Northeast Coastal and Barrier Network (NCBN) monitors seagrass conditions over time, especially as concerns rise about the effects of warming waters.

In recent decades, eelgrass along the Northeast U.S. coast has experienced water temperatures that approach or exceed known physiological limits. When water becomes too warm, eelgrass respiration increases faster than photosynthesis, creating stress on the plants. Studies show that temperatures above 25 °C increase the likelihood of eelgrass mortality, and sustained exposure above 30 °C has led to complete die offs in areas such as Chesapeake Bay ([Jarvis & Moore 2010](#refs); [Plaisted et al. 2022](#refs), [Novak et al. 2023](#refs)). Long term temperature records from New Hampshire to Maryland indicate that warmer summers are consistently followed by lower eelgrass presence in the next year ([Plaisted et al. 2022](#refs)). Temperature stress is also more severe when combined with poor water clarity, low light, or high nutrient levels. Repeated hot summers without adequate recovery time can lead to large scale declines.

These patterns align with broader climate driven trends. Marine heatwaves, periods when water temperatures exceed the 90^th^ percentile of a 30 year baseline, have become more frequent and more intense ([Berger et al. 2024](#refs)). Such events have already affected corals, kelp forests, and seagrasses worldwide. Research shows that eelgrass meadows experiencing both high heat and little cooling tend to lose shoot density and canopy height, while sites that receive periodic cooling are better able to recover between heat events ([Berger et al. 2024](#refs)).

Other marine systems use cumulative heat stress metrics to understand these risks. NOAA’s Degree Heating Weeks (DHW) index, for example, tracks how much and how long temperatures exceed thresholds that lead to coral bleaching and mortality ([Skirving et al. 2020](#refs)). These tools emphasize that it is the accumulation of heat over time, not single temperature spikes, that often drive ecosystem decline. This same concept is directly relevant to eelgrass.

Calculating cumulative stress frameworks requires long term temperature datasets. To better understand how warming affects eelgrass within the Northeast Coastal and Barrier Network parks, we used long term temperature datasets to:

1.  Identify years with extreme heat and low cooling,
2.  Determine where these conditions overlapped to create compounded “stress years,” and
3.  Compare patterns across parks to understand regional vulnerability.

# Methods

## Protocol

The Northeast Coastal and Barrier Network uses the Tier-3 standardized Estuarine Nutrient Enrichment monitoring protocol ([Kopp and Neckles, 2009](#refs)) that includes seagrass condition monitoring (SOP 9). Seagrass condition is characterized using measures of population and shoot structure to track how meadows change. This protocol was adapted from the international SeagrassNet program that provides a framework for standardized seagrass monitoring worldwide.

Within the network, three coastal parks, Assateague Island National Seashore (ASIS), Cape Cod National Seashore, and Fire Island National Seashore (FIIS) implement the Tier-3 seagrass monitoring protocol. At `r params$park`, long-term seagrass condition is monitored at `r as.character(english::english(n_distinct(dat_pk$Site_Name[dat_pk$long_term_site == TRUE])))` site`r if (n_distinct(dat_pk$Site_Name) > 1) {'s'}`: `r knitr::combine_words(unique(dat_pk$Site_Name[dat_pk$long_term_site == TRUE]))`. `r if_else(params$park == "CACO", paste("Additionally, water temperature data was also collected at", knitr::combine_words(unique(dat_pk$Site_Name[dat_pk$long_term_site == FALSE])), "beginning in 2023 to provide broader ecological context, but these sites are not included in the long-term monitoring protocol."), "")`

At each long-term monitoring site, seagrass condition is monitored across three permanent 50-m transects that run parallel to shore at a shallow, mid-depth, and deep depth. `r if_else(params$park == "CACO", paste("Note that at East Harbor and Provincetown Harbor, temperature data was only collected at a single depth: XX meters.", ""), "")`Temperature loggers (HOBO UTBI-001 Tidbit Temp, Onset Computer Corporation) are attached to each transect and record water temperature at continuous 15-minute intervals throughout the year.

Following data collection, water temperature data from each year are uploaded to the NPS AQUARIUS WebPortal for QAQC, certification, and storage. Additional details about data collection at each site are shown in Table 1 below.

```{r Table1, echo=FALSE}

# Use your existing site_summary() and filter to the selected park
site_sum <- dat_pk %>%
  group_by(Park_Code, Site_Name) %>%
  summarise(
      n_years = dplyr::n_distinct(Year),
      first_year = min(Year, na.rm = TRUE),
      last_year = max(Year, na.rm = TRUE),
      is_longterm_site = if_else(any(long_term_site) == TRUE, "Yes", "No"),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
  arrange(Site_Name) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select("Site name" = Site_Name, "First year of data" = first_year, "Most recent year of data" = last_year, "Total years of data" = n_years, "Long-term monitoring site" = is_longterm_site) %>%
  {if (params$park != "CACO")
    select(., -c(`Long-term monitoring site`))
    else .}

# Clean, simple table using only knitr::kable()
kable(
  site_sum,
  caption = paste0(
    "Table 1. Site-specific details for temperature data available from each site in ",
    params$park,
    " after data filters were applied."
  ),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  label = NA,
  align = "c"
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE
    ) %>%
  row_spec(0, extra_css = "border-top: 2px solid lightgrey;") %>%
  row_spec(nrow(site_sum), extra_css = "border-bottom: 1px solid lightgrey")
```

## Data Summerization & Analysis

For this analysis, we focused on the continuous water temperature data and examined patterns of thermal stress and cooling dynamics with the goals of understanding the thermal conditions at `r params$park` and comparing with those at other long-term monitoring sites at `r knitr::combine_words(unique(dat %>% filter(Park_Code != params$park) %>% pull(Park_Code)))`.

Prior to analyses, temperature data were first filtered to the months from May-September to hone in on when thermal stress likely occurs. To ensure that the data from each sampling year occurred consistently over the same time periods, we then further filtered the data from each site to only include years with 80% data coverage and with at least 10 days of data in a minimum of three of the five focal months. Then, data from the individual transects at each site were summarized up to a single thermal metric value (HDH or CDH) for each year as described in the next section.

### Novel Thermal Metrics for Eelgrass

For eelgrass, prolonged exposure to high temperatures can lead to physiological stress where respiration outpaces photosynthesis. To describe long-term thermal conditions in eelgrass meadows, we calculated two site-level yearly metrics from the continuous water temperature data: **heating degree hours (HDH)** and **cooling degree hours (CDH)** ([Berger et al. 2024](#refs)). HDH is calculated by first summing the total number of hours above a park-specific temperature threshold across the focal months (May to September) to get the total HDH per year. The total HDH values are then divided by the sum of all hours in the period of record for each year to get the mean HDH per year. Similarly, mean CDH for each year is calculated using the same method but for the number of hours below the site-specific temperature threshold. We applied a threshold of 25 °C for northern sites (CACO and FIIS) and 28.6 °C for southern sites (ASIS) ([Berger & Berg 2024](#refs)) based on known values of eelgrass thermal tolerance reported in peer-reviewed literature. Thus, these metrics capture not just how hot or cold the water gets, but how long water temperatures remain above or below a biological threshold. When examined together, HDH and CDH help to understand the factors controlling eelgrass loss and recovery across coastal National Parks in the northeast (Figure 1).

```{r concepts, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.show='hold', fig.width=4, fig.height=3, fig.cap="Figure 1. Conceptualized diagrams demonstrating how yearly heating degree hours (HDH) and cooling degree hours (CDH) are calculated for each site."}
### HDH and CDH Concept##

threshold <- if_else(params$park == "ASIS", 28.5, 25)

df <- tibble(
  time = 1:100,
  temp = threshold + sin(time/10)*3
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = time, y = temp)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = threshold, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = df %>% filter(temp > threshold),
    aes(ymin = threshold, ymax = temp),
    fill = "red",
    alpha = 0.4
  ) +
  labs(title = "HDH = Heat Above Threshold") +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_classic()

ggplot(df, aes(x = time, y = temp)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = threshold, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = df %>% filter(temp < threshold),
    aes(ymin = temp, ymax = threshold),
    fill = "blue",
    alpha = 0.4
  ) +
  labs(title = "CDH = Cooling Below Threshold") +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_classic()


```

### Site-Specific Extremes

Within each site at `r params$park`, we identified extreme high heat years as those years where the mean HDH was above the site-specific 90^th^ percentile (`r paste0(threshold, "°C")`). These years represent years when the eelgrass meadow at these sites experienced the most intense and prolonged thermal stress. Similarly, we also identified extreme low cooling years as those years where the mean CDH was above the 90^th^ percentile for each site. In this context, lower CDH values (i.e., more negative) represent greater cooling below the temperature threshold at each site, indicating potential periods of relief for eelgrass meadows. Finally, we identified compound stress years as those years with both extreme high heat and extreme low cooling.

### Regional Comparisons

In order to understand regional patterns of seagrass thermal stress and cooling relief among coastal parks in the northeast, we used k-means cluster analysis to categorize each site in ASIS, CACO, and FIIS into four thermal stress regimes and four cooling relief regimes. K-means cluster analyses were conducted using 4 centers (to represent the 4 thermal stress or cooling relief regimes) and 2 random sets via the algorithm defined by [Hartigan and Wong](#refs) as implemented in the 'kmeans' function from the 'stats' package in R.

The four thermal stress regimes were based on 1) the % time above the previously mentioned park-specific threshold averaged across all years (i.e., duration of warming), and 2) the number of warming events averaged across all years (i.e., frequency of warming). Thermal stress regimes were defined as:

-   **Episodic Heatwave**: Sites with infrequent, but intense heat events
-   **Highly Stressed**: Sites with frequent and prolonged heat events
-   **Low Exposure**: Sites with infrequent and limited time above thermal threshold
-   **Chronic Warming**: Sites with frequent heat events that are not prolonged (pulsing)

The four cooling relief regimes were based on 1) the mean cooling degree hours averaged across all years at each site, and 2) the mean heating degree hours averaged across all years at each site. Cooling relief regimes were defined as:

-   **Stable Systems**: Low heat stress and moderate cooling
-   **Chronic Heat Stress**: High heat stress and limited cooling
-   **Thermal Refugia**: Low heat stress and strong cooling
-   **Stress Buffered Systems**: High heat stress but strong cooling

# Results

## Extreme High Heat Years

```{r extreme-HDH-years, echo=FALSE}
# Prepare site-year data for the selected park
site_year <- dat %>%
  filter(Park_Code == params$park) %>%
  group_by(Site_Name, Year) %>%
  summarise(Mean_HDH = mean(Mean_HDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Use your helper to flag extreme years (90th percentile)
site_year_extreme <- flag_extreme_by_site(site_year, value_col = "Mean_HDH", prob = 0.9, flag_name = "Extreme") %>%
  filter(Extreme) %>%
  arrange(Site_Name, Year)

site_result_text <- function(site_year_extreme_df, metric, park_code) {
  
  sites <- unique(dat_pk$Site_Name)
  
  extreme_sites <- site_year_extreme_df %>%
    {if (metric == "HDH" | metric == "CDH")
      filter(., Extreme)
      else if (metric == "double_stress")
        filter(., Double_Stress)
    } %>%
    group_by(Site_Name) %>%
    summarise(years = as.character(knitr::combine_words(Year))) %>%
    mutate(text = paste(years, "for", Site_Name))
  
  no_extreme_sites <- sites[(!sites %in% extreme_sites$Site_Name)]
  
  extreme_sites_text <- if (metric == "HDH") {
    paste0("Extreme high heat years were ", knitr::combine_words(extreme_sites$text), " (Figure 2).")
  } else if (metric == "CDH") {
    paste0("Extreme low cooling years were ", knitr::combine_words(extreme_sites$text), " (Figure 3).")
  } else if (metric == "double_stress") {
    paste0("Compound stress years were ", knitr::combine_words(extreme_sites$text), " (Figure 4).")
  }
  
  all_sites_text <- case_when(
    length(no_extreme_sites) == 0 ~ extreme_sites_text,
    
    length(no_extreme_sites) >= 1 & length(no_extreme_sites) < length(sites) & metric == "HDH" ~ paste0(extreme_sites_text, " There were no extreme high heat years at ", knitr::combine_words(no_extreme_sites, and = " or "), "."),
    length(no_extreme_sites) >= 1 & length(no_extreme_sites) < length(sites) & metric == "CDH" ~ paste0(extreme_sites_text, " There were no extreme low cooling years at ", knitr::combine_words(no_extreme_sites, and = " or "), "."),
    length(no_extreme_sites) >= 1 & length(no_extreme_sites) < length(sites) & metric == "double_stress" ~ paste0(extreme_sites_text, " There were no compound stress years at ", knitr::combine_words(no_extreme_sites, and = " or "), "."),
    
    length(no_extreme_sites) == length(sites) & metric == "HDH" ~ paste0("There were no extreme high heat years at any of the sites at ", park_code, " (Figure 2)."),
    length(no_extreme_sites) == length(sites) & metric == "CDH" ~ paste0("There were no extreme low cooling years at any of the sites at ", park_code, " (Figure 3)."),
    length(no_extreme_sites) == length(sites) & metric == "double_stress" ~ paste0("There were no compound stress years at any of the sites at ", park_code, " (Figure 4).")
  )
  
  return(all_sites_text)
}
```

`r site_result_text(site_year_extreme, "HDH", params$park)`

```{r plot-hdh, fig.width=10, fig.height=8, fig.cap=paste0("Figure 2. Mean yearly HDH for each site at ", params$park, ". Extreme high heat years (red points) are years when mean HDH exceeded the site-specific 90th percentile value (red dashed line)."), echo=FALSE}
plot_hdh_extreme_by_site(dat, park_code)
```

## Extreme Low Cooling Years

```{r extreme-CDH-years, echo=FALSE}

# Prepare site-year data for the selected park
site_year_cdh <- dat %>%
  filter(Park_Code == params$park) %>%
  group_by(Site_Name, Year) %>%
  summarise(Mean_CDH = mean(Mean_CDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Use your helper to flag extreme low cooling years (90th percentile)
site_year_cdh_extreme <- flag_extreme_by_site(site_year_cdh, value_col = "Mean_CDH", prob = 0.9, flag_name = "Extreme") %>%
  filter(Extreme) %>%
  arrange(Site_Name, Year)
```

`r site_result_text(site_year_cdh_extreme, "CDH", params$park)`

```{r plot-cdh, fig.width=10, fig.height=8, fig.cap=paste0("Figure 3. Mean yearly CDH for each site at ", params$park, ". Extreme low cooling years (blue points) are years when mean CDH exceeded the site-specific 90th percentile value (blue dashed line)."), echo=FALSE}
plot_cdh_lowcool_by_site(dat, park_code)
```

## Compound Stress Years

```{r double-stress-years, echo=FALSE}

# Summarize double‑stress years (HDH high & CDH high) for each site in the selected park

double_stress <- dat %>%
  filter(Park_Code == params$park) %>%
  group_by(Site_Name, Year) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean_HDH = mean(Mean_HDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE),
    Mean_CDH = mean(Mean_CDH_daily, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  group_by(Site_Name) %>%
  mutate(
    hdh_thresh = quantile(Mean_HDH, 0.9, na.rm = TRUE),
    cdh_thresh = quantile(Mean_CDH, 0.9, na.rm = TRUE),
    High_Heat   = Mean_HDH >= hdh_thresh,
    Low_Cooling = Mean_CDH >= cdh_thresh,   # high CDH = low cooling
    Double_Stress = High_Heat & Low_Cooling
  ) %>%
  arrange(Site_Name, Year)
```

`r site_result_text(double_stress, "double_stress", params$park)`

```{r plot-stress, fig.width=10, fig.height=8, fig.cap="Figure 4. Compound stress years (purple points) are years when mean CDH **and** mean HDH exceeded the site-specific 90th percentile values.", echo=FALSE}
plot_double_stress_by_site(dat,park_code)
```

## How does `r params$park` compare across the region?

```{r regional-results, echo=FALSE, fig.width=10, fig.height=6, fig.cap="Figure 5. Heat event structure across three coastal National Park Service units (CACO, ASIS, FIIS). Each point represents a site-level average of the mean number of warming events per year (frequency) and the mean percentage of time above the thermal threshold (duration). Dashed lines divide the figure into four interpretive thermal regimes. Labels show the average number of monitoring years contributing to each site-level estimate."}
plot_heat_event_structure_site(dat)
```

Across the three coastal NCBN parks, clear regional patterns emerge. At CACO, most sites fall in the category of having fewer and shorter heat‑stress events. An exception is the partially restored East Harbor system, which shows a different pattern. Overall, CACO sites tend to experience heat stress as brief or infrequent pulses rather than long, sustained events.

In contrast, Tingles Island at ASIS experiences heat stress much more often, and these events add up throughout the season. This pattern is expected for parks located in warmer parts of the region, where longer and more frequent heat events are common.

FIIS sites fall between these two extremes. Heat‑stress conditions vary widely across the park, depending on where each site is located. For example, Moriches Bay shows short, frequent warming events, while Great South Bay experiences longer or more sustained heat episodes. This mix reflects the diversity of habitats and local conditions across Fire Island.

These results show that sites across the regional scale experience different heat stress conditions and even sites within the same park respond in different ways. This raises an important questions that will require further data collection and analysis: **how are some of the most chronically stressed sites, like Moriches Bay, Pleasant Bay, and Tingles Island still able to support eelgrass populations?**

## What are the impacts of cooling?

Now that we've seen sites experience heat stress differently, we can look at cooling relief from heat events. Here, we divide the eelgrass into the four previously described cooling regimes based on the balance between thermal stress and thermal relief:

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.width=12, fig.height=6, fig.cap="Figure 6. Thermal stress and cooling recovery across three coastal National Park Service units (CACO, ASIS, FIIS). Each point represents a site‑level average of the mean heating degree hours per day (thermal stress) and the mean cooling degree hours per day (thermal recovery). Dashed lines divide the figure into four interpretive recovery regimes. Labels show the average number of monitoring years contributing to each site‑level estimate."}
plot_stress_vs_cooling(dat)
```

The balance between heat stress events and cooling recovery also varies across the region and within parks. Pleasant Bay and Moriches Bay receive some of the strongest cooling periods among the chronically warm sites, second only to the fully open tidal systems on Cape Cod. This pattern reflects their position relative to inlets where tidal flushing brings in cooler ocean water that helps moderate warmer temperatures and sustain populations of eelgrass. In contrast, sites with more limited flushing, like Great South Bay and East Harbor, show weaker cooling relief consistent with restricted or partially restored flow. These sites match site conditions that no longer support eelgrass. Tingles Island falls between these two outcomes as it receives little cooling, sits between a stable and thermal refugia zone, and supports sparse eelgrass populations.

# Key Takeaways

DRAFT

# References

Berger, A.C. & Berg, P. (2024) Eelgrass meadow response to heat stress. I. Temperature threshold for ecosystem production derived from in situ aquatic eddy covariance measurements. Marine Ecology Progress Series 736:35-46. <https://doi.org/10.3354/meps14587>

Berger A.C., Berg P., McGlathery K.J., Aoki L.R., & Kerns K. (2024) Eelgrass meadow response to heat stress. II. Impacts of ocean warming and marine heatwaves measured by novel metrics. Marine Ecology Progress Series 736:47-62. <https://doi.org/10.3354/meps14588>

Hartigan, J.A. & Wong, M.A. (1979) Algorithm AS 136: A k-means clustering algorithm. Applied Statistics 28:100-108. <https://doi.org/10.2307/2346830>

Jarvis, J.C. & Moore, K.A. (2010) The role of the photosynthetic pathway in the acclimation of Zostera marina to light and temperature stress. Journal of Experimental Marine Biology and Ecology 395:105–110.

Kopp, B.S., & Neckles, H.A. (2009) A protocol for monitoring estuarine nutrient enrichment in coastal parks of the National Park Service Northeast Region. Natural Resource Report NPS/NCBN/NRR—2009/110. National Park Service, Fort Collins, Colorado. <https://irma.nps.gov/DataStore/Reference/Profile/661689>

Novak, A.B., Plaisted, H.K., Hughes, Z.J., Mittermayr, A., & Molden, E. (2023) Eelgrass (<i>Zostera marina L.</i>) populations are threatened by high sea-surface temperatures and impaired waters on Nantucket Island, USA. Marine Pollution Bulletin 197:115689. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpolbul.2023.115689>

Orth, R.J., Carruthers, T.J., Dennison, W.C., Duarte, C.M., Fourqurean, J.W., Heck, K.L., Hughes, A.R., Kendrick, G.A., Kenworthy, W.J., Olyarnik, S., Short, F.T., Waycott, M. & Williams, S. L. (2006). A global crisis for seagrass ecosystems. BioScience 56:987-996. [https://doi.org/10.1641/0006-3568(2006)56[987:AGCFSE]2.0.CO;2](https://doi.org/10.1641/0006-3568(2006)56%5B987:AGCFSE%5D2.0.CO;2){.uri}

Plaisted, H.K., Shields, E.C., Novak, A.B., Peck, C.P., Schenck, F., Carr, J., Duffy, P.A., Evans, N.T., Fox, S.E., Heck, S.M., Hudson, R., Mattera, T., Moore, K.A., Neikirk, B., Parrish, D.B., Peterson, B.J., Short, F.T. & Tinoco, A.I. (2022) Influence of rising water temperature on the temperate seagrass species eelgrass (<i>Zostera marina L.</i>) in the Northeast USA. Frontiers in Marine Science 9:920699. <https://doi.org/10.3389/fmars.2022.920699>

Skirving, W., Marsh, B., De La Cour, J., Liu, G., Harris, A., Maturi, E., Geiger, E. & Eakin, C.M. (2020) CoralTemp and the coral reef watch coral bleaching heat stress product suite version 3.1. Remote Sensing 12:3856. <https://doi.org/10.3390/rs12233856>

## Contact Information

| Katie Button, NCBN Biologist
| Kaitlyn_Button\@nps.gov
| 
| Holly Plaisted, NCBN Biologist & Seagrass Protocol Lead
| Holly_Plaisted\@nps.gov
| 
| [Vital Signs: Northeastern Coastal & Barrier Inventory and Monitoring Network](https://www.nps.gov/im/ncbn/what-we-monitor.htm)
| [Reports & Publications (U.S. National Park Service) (nps.gov)](https://www.nps.gov/im/ncbn/reports-publications-data.htm)
